---
title: "Week 2: Sensor Presentation"
subtitle: "Focus on Landsat 8/9 (OLI & TIRS)"
date: "2026-01-24"
---

## 1. Sensor Presentation

### 1.1 Reproducible Presentation Workflow

I built this presentation using the **xaringan** R package, which lets you write slides in Markdown and embed live code alongside the content [@grolemund2022]. The main reason for choosing it was **reproducibility** — keeping the analysis and the presentation in the same code-based workflow means the two stay in sync rather than drifting apart.

### 1.2 Slide Deck Access and Interaction

The full interactive presentation is hosted on **GitHub Pages**. The embedded frame below lets you view the slides directly on the page, and the link in the callout opens a full-screen version in a separate tab.

::: {.callout-note}
### Slide Deck Access
Below is my embedded slide deck on the **Landsat** program. You can view the slides here or [click here for the full-screen version](https://brotree-code.github.io/casa0023-presentation/).
:::

```{r echo = FALSE, out.width='100%', fig.align='center'}
# Using knitr to embed the Xaringan HTML presentation into Quarto
knitr::include_url("https://brotree-code.github.io/casa0023-presentation/")
```

---

## 2. Content Summary

### 2.1 Horizontal Comparison with Sentinel-2

Sentinel-2 has finer **spatial resolution** in several key optical bands, but Landsat 8/9 offers stronger **long-term consistency** at 30m. The more distinctive advantage of Landsat is its **thermal sensor** — Sentinel-2 does not carry one, which makes Landsat more useful for urban thermal studies.

### 2.2 Satellite Payloads and Instrumentation

Landsat 8/9 carries two separate instruments:

* **OLI (Operational Land Imager)** — covers visible, near-infrared, and shortwave infrared wavelengths
* **TIRS (Thermal Infrared Sensor)** — records thermal data used for land surface temperature analysis

That combination — optical and thermal in one platform — is what makes Landsat particularly useful for urban heat studies.

![Figure 1: Schematic of the Landsat 8-9 satellite platform highlighting the **OLI** and **TIRS** sensor payloads. Source: NASA.](images/landsat_sensor.jpg)

### 2.3 Urban Research Significance

Landsat's thermal band (Band 10) makes it possible to estimate **Land Surface Temperature (LST)**, which is the basis for **Urban Heat Island (UHI)** analysis. This feeds directly into **urban planning** and heat-related policy work — exactly the kind of application that came up in the week 4/5 Ahmedabad case study.

![Figure 2: Spectral band comparison between **Landsat 8/9 OLI/TIRS** and **Sentinel-2 MSI** sensors.](images/band_comparison.png)

| Key Feature | **Landsat 8/9 (OLI & TIRS)** | **Sentinel-2 (MSI)** | Implications for Urban Research |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| **Optical Resolution** | 30 m | 10 m – 60 m | Sentinel-2 is more suitable for detailed vegetation mapping at street level. |
| **Thermal Infrared** | **Yes** | No | Landsat is more suitable for heat mapping because it includes thermal bands. |
| **Data Archive** | Since 1972 | Since 2015 | Landsat is one of the most important sources for tracking long-term urban expansion. |

---

## 3. Applications

### 3.1 Environmental Indices and Monitoring

NDVI is a straightforward starting point for assessing urban **green space** — it uses the ratio of **near-infrared** and **red reflectance** to measure vegetation presence and condition. Beyond vegetation, the same spectral data can be used to examine how **green infrastructure** affects urban temperatures, since vegetated surfaces generally stay cooler than hard surfaces.

$$NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$$

### 3.2 Spectral Signature Integrity

Working with spectral signatures involves selecting the right bands for the study area, then comparing reflectance curves across land cover types — for instance, distinguishing asphalt from vegetation by how differently they reflect across the spectrum. Validation against ground-truth data or high-resolution imagery helps confirm that the resulting classifications are reliable.

::: {.callout-important}
### Spectral Signature Analysis
A key practical issue is that spectral signatures are not fixed — the same land cover can look different depending on the season, sensor, or atmospheric conditions. This is why comparing curves from multiple dates or sensors, rather than relying on a single reference, tends to give more reliable classifications.
:::

![Figure 3: Spectral signature curves of typical land covers in Guiyang generated in R (with standard deviation error bars).](images/spectral_reflectance2.png)

### 3.3 Identifying Urban Hot Spots

Different surface materials absorb and release heat at different rates — **concrete and asphalt** store more heat than grass, which shows up clearly in thermal imagery. Areas with dense **built-up land** tend to sit noticeably warmer than surrounding areas, and mapping that pattern is the starting point for understanding the spatial structure of the **UHI effect**.

![Figure 4: Urban Hot Spots](images/uhi_map.png)

---

## 4. Personal Reflection

### 4.1 Earth Observation and Open Data

The open **Landsat archive** has made a real difference for long-term environmental monitoring — the fact that imagery going back to **1972** is freely available is something I did not fully appreciate before this week. Combining Landsat’s **thermal data** with Sentinel-2’s finer spatial detail seems like an obvious direction for urban **microclimate** work, and something I would like to try in a future project.

### 4.2 Technical Evolution and Workflow

Building the xaringan presentation was harder than expected — learning Markdown and CSS at the same time while also thinking about content took a while. Moving toward a Quarto-based workflow [@kirenz_nd] felt like a natural next step: combining code and written explanation in one document makes it easier to keep the analysis and the write-up in sync [@xie2022]. The reproducibility angle matters to me practically, not just in principle — if I want to revisit this analysis in a year, I need to be able to follow my own steps [@quarto_quickstart].

::: {.callout-caution}
### Best Practice: Project Separation
As discussed in the module, it is generally better not to carry out complex analysis and portfolio writing in the same R project, because keeping them separate helps maintain clarity and organisation.
:::

![Figure 5: Screenshot of the RStudio workspace used for portfolio writing, showing the active document and the project file structure.](images/technical_workflow.png)

---

## 5. References

::: {#refs}
:::
